translations.idiomatic: Cen Peng, Geng Yan, Jia Fu, Fu Jun, Zang Gong, and the rest pursued Deng Feng to Xiao Chang'an, where the emperor himself led the assault and crushed him.

sentences.zh: 奉迫急，乃降。

translations.literal: Feng, pressed tight, thereupon surrendered.
translations.idiomatic: Hard pressed, Deng Feng surrendered.

sentences.zh: 帝憐奉舊功臣，且釁起吳漢，欲全宥之。

translations.literal: The emperor pitied Feng as an old meritorious minister, and moreover the provocation arose from Wu Han; he wished wholly to forgive him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor pitied an old servant of the cause and knew Wu Han had provoked the revolt; he meant to spare Deng Feng.

sentences.zh: 彭與耿弇諫曰：『鄧奉背恩反逆，暴師經年，致賈復傷痍，朱祐見獲。

translations.literal: Peng with Geng Yan remonstrated, saying: 'Deng Feng turned his back on grace and rebelled; he exposed the army for a full year, causing Jia Fu to be wounded and Zhu You to be captured.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Peng and Geng Yan objected: 'Deng Feng betrayed your kindness, kept the armies in the field a year, wounded Jia Fu, and captured Zhu You.'

sentences.zh: 陛下既至，不知悔善，而親在行陳，兵敗乃降。

translations.literal: Since Your Majesty has arrived, he has not known to repent and do good, but personally was in the battle line; only when the troops were defeated did he surrender.
translations.idiomatic: Even after you arrived he showed no remorse, fought you in person, and only yielded when beaten.'

sentences.zh: 若不誅奉，無以懲惡。

translations.literal: If Feng is not executed, there is no means to punish evil.
translations.idiomatic: Spare him and you cannot warn the wicked.'

sentences.zh: 』於是斬之。

translations.literal: ' Thereupon he beheaded him.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Feng was executed.

sentences.zh: 奉者，西華侯鄧晨之兄子也。

translations.literal: Feng was the nephew by elder brother of Marquis Xihua Deng Chen.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Feng was a nephew of Deng Chen, marquis of Xihua.

sentences.zh: 車駕引還，令彭率傅俊、臧宮、劉宏等三萬餘人南擊秦豐，拔黃郵，豐與其大將蔡宏拒彭等於鄧，數月不得進。

translations.literal: The imperial carriage led the return and ordered Peng to lead Fu Jun, Zang Gong, Liu Hong, and others, more than thirty thousand men, south to strike Qin Feng; he took Huangyou; Feng with his great general Cai Hong resisted Peng and the others at Deng and for several months could not advance.
translations.idiomatic: On the withdrawal the emperor gave Cen Peng Fu Jun, Zang Gong, Liu Hong, and thirty thousand men to strike Qin Feng, seized Huangyou, then stalled at Deng against Qin Feng and Cai Hong for months.

sentences.zh: 帝怪以讓彭，彭懼，於是夜勒兵馬，申令軍中，使明旦西擊山都。

translations.literal: The emperor wondered and reproached Peng; Peng was afraid; thereupon by night he tightened horses and troops, issued orders in the army, and made them next morning strike Shandu to the west.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor rebuked the delay, Cen Peng—alarmed—drilled his troops by night and announced a dawn march west against Shandu.

sentences.zh: 乃緩所獲虜，令得逃亡，歸以告豐，豐即采其軍西邀彭。

translations.literal: He then relaxed the captives taken so they could flee and return to report to Feng; Feng immediately gathered his army west to intercept Peng.
translations.idiomatic: He released prisoners to carry word to Qin Feng, who at once shifted his army west to intercept Cen Peng.

sentences.zh: 彭乃潛兵度沔水，擊其將張楊於阿頭山，大破之。

translations.literal: Peng thereupon secretly led troops across the Mian River and struck his general Zhang Yang at Mount Atou, greatly defeating him.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Peng meanwhile slipped across the Han River, fell on Zhang Yang at Mount Atou, and routed him.

sentences.zh: 從川谷間伐木開道，直襲黎丘，擊破諸屯兵。

translations.literal: From among the streams and valleys he cut wood and opened a road, went straight to raid Liqiu, and broke the various encamped troops.
translations.idiomatic: He cut a track through the valleys, burst into Liqiu, and tore apart the outlying camps.

sentences.zh: 豐聞大驚，馳歸救之。

translations.literal: When Feng heard, he was greatly alarmed and galloped back to rescue it.
translations.idiomatic: Qin Feng raced back to save his base.

sentences.zh: 彭與諸將依東山為營，豐與蔡宏夜攻鼓，彭豫為之備，出兵逆擊之，豐敗走，追斬蔡宏。

translations.literal: Peng with the various generals relied on the eastern mountain to make a camp; Feng with Cai Hong attacked by night the drums; Peng had prepared for it in advance, sent troops to meet and strike them; Feng was defeated and fled; they pursued and beheaded Cai Hong.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Peng's line stood on the eastern hills; Qin Feng and Cai Hong attacked by night, but Cen Peng was ready, counterattacked, drove Qin Feng off, and killed Cai Hong in the pursuit.

sentences.zh: 更封彭為舞陰侯。

translations.literal: His fief was changed and Peng was made marquis of Wuyin.
translations.idiomatic: His fief was moved to the marquisate of Wuyin.

sentences.zh: 秦豐相趙京舉宜城降，拜為成漢將軍，與彭共圍豐於黎丘。

translations.literal: Qin Feng's chancellor Zhao Jing raised Yicheng and surrendered; he was appointed general who completes Han and with Peng jointly besieged Feng at Liqiu.
translations.idiomatic: Qin Feng's chancellor Zhao Jing surrendered Yicheng and was named general who completes Han; together they tightened the siege of Liqiu.

sentences.zh: 時田戎擁眾夷陵，聞秦豐被圍，懼大兵方至，欲降。

translations.literal: At that time Tian Rong held a host at Yiling; hearing that Qin Feng was besieged, he feared the great army was about to arrive and wished to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Rong of Yiling, hearing Qin Feng surrounded, feared the main host would reach him next and wished to submit.

sentences.zh: 而妻兄辛臣諫戎曰：『今四方豪傑各據郡國，洛陽地如掌耳，不如按甲以觀其變。

translations.literal: But his wife's elder brother Xin Chen remonstrated with Rong, saying: 'Now heroes of the four quarters each hold commanderies and states; Luoyang is like the palm of a hand—it is better to hold armor and observe the changes.
translations.idiomatic: His brother-in-law Xin Chen argued, 'Every warlord still clutches a domain; Luoyang itself commands only a palmful of ground—better keep your spears and watch how the wind shifts.'

sentences.zh: 』戎曰：『以秦王之強，猶為征南所圍，豈況吾邪？

translations.literal: ' Rong said: 'With the Qin king's strength, still he is besieged by the southern expedition—how much more I?
translations.idiomatic: Tian Rong answered, 'Even mighty Qin Feng is trapped by the southern commander—what chance have I?'

sentences.zh: 降計決矣。

translations.literal: The plan to surrender is settled.
translations.idiomatic: My mind is made up to yield.'

sentences.zh: 』四年春，戎乃留辛臣守夷陵，自將兵沿江溯沔止黎丘，刻期日當降，而辛臣於後盜戎珍寶，從間道先降於彭，而以書招戎。

translations.literal: ' In the spring of the fourth year Rong thereupon left Xin Chen to guard Yiling, himself led troops along the river up the Mian to stop at Liqiu, and fixed a day when he should surrender; but Xin Chen in the rear stole Rong's precious goods, first surrendered to Peng by a secret path, and with a letter summoned Rong.
translations.idiomatic: In the fourth spring Tian Rong left Xin Chen at Yiling while he sailed up the Han toward Liqiu to surrender on a set date—until Xin Chen stole his treasures, slipped to Cen Peng ahead of him, and wrote urging him to come over.

sentences.zh: 戎疑必賣己，遂不敢降，百反與秦豐合，彭出兵攻戎，數月，大破之，其大將伍公詣彭降，戎亡歸夷陵。

translations.literal: Rong suspected he would certainly sell him and thereupon did not dare surrender; instead he turned and joined with Qin Feng; Peng sent troops to attack Rong; after several months he greatly defeated him; his great general Wu Gong went to Peng and surrendered; Rong fled and returned to Yiling.
translations.idiomatic: Suspecting betrayal, Tian Rong refused to yield and instead rejoined Qin Feng; Cen Peng attacked him for months, broke his army, won over his general Wu Gong, and drove Tian Rong back to Yiling.

sentences.zh: 帝幸黎丘勞軍，封彭吏士有功者百餘人。

translations.literal: The emperor visited Liqiu to encourage the army and enfeoffed more than a hundred of Peng's officers and soldiers who had merit.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor visited Liqiu to hearten the troops and ennobled over a hundred of Cen Peng's soldiers.

sentences.zh: 彭攻秦豐三歲，斬首九萬餘級，豐餘兵裁千人，又城中食且盡。

translations.literal: Peng attacked Qin Feng three years, beheading more than ninety thousand; Feng's remaining troops were barely a thousand men, and moreover food in the city was nearly exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: Three years of siege cost Qin Feng ninety thousand dead; fewer than a thousand defenders remained and the city was nearly out of food.

sentences.zh: 帝以豐轉弱，令朱祐代彭守之，使彭與傅俊南擊田戎，大破之，遂拔夷陵，追至秭歸。

translations.literal: The emperor, because Feng had grown weaker, ordered Zhu You to replace Peng in guarding him and sent Peng with Fu Jun south to strike Tian Rong, greatly defeated him, thereupon took Yiling, and pursued to Zigui.
translations.idiomatic: Seeing Qin Feng enfeebled, he left Zhu You to finish the siege while Cen Peng and Fu Jun struck Tian Rong, took Yiling, and chased him to Zigui.

sentences.zh: 戎與數十騎亡入蜀，盡獲其妻子士眾數萬人。

translations.literal: Rong with several tens of cavalry fled into Shu; his wife, children, officers, and host to the number of several tens of thousands were all captured.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Rong fled into Shu with a few dozen horsemen; his family, officers, and tens of thousands of soldiers were taken.

sentences.zh: 彭以將伐蜀漢，而夾川谷少，水險難漕運，留威虜將軍馮駿軍江州，都尉田鴻軍夷陵，領軍李玄軍夷道，自引兵還屯津鄉，當荊州要會，喻告諸蠻夷，降者奏封其君長。

translations.literal: Peng, because he was about to campaign against Shu-Han and the river gorges were narrow with little water, the danger of water making transport by canal difficult, left general who awes the barbarians Feng Jun with troops at Jiangzhou, commandant Tian Hong with troops at Yiling, army commander Li Xuan with troops at Yidao, himself led troops back to encamp at Jinxiang, blocked the critical junction of Jingzhou, proclaimed and informed the various barbarians, and those who surrendered he memorialized to enfeoff their lords and chiefs.
translations.idiomatic: Preparing to invade Shu, Cen Peng stationed Feng Jun at Jiangzhou, Tian Hong at Yiling, Li Xuan at Yidao, and returned to Jinxiang to hold the throat of Jingzhou, while edicts promised titles to any tribal chief who submitted.

sentences.zh: 初，彭與交阯牧鄧讓厚善，與讓書陳國家威德，又遣偏將軍屈充移檄江南，班行詔命。

translations.literal: At first Peng had been on thickly good terms with Jiaozhi shepherd Deng Rang; he sent Rang a letter stating the state's might and virtue, and also sent lieutenant general Qu Chong to move proclamations south of the Yangzi and circulate edicts and orders.
translations.idiomatic: He had long been friendly with Jiaozhi governor Deng Rang; he wrote of Han's power and sent Lieutenant General Qu Chong through the south with the emperor's proclamations.

sentences.zh: 於是讓與江夏太守侯登、武陵太守王堂、長沙相韓福、桂陽太守張隆、零陵太守田翕、蒼梧太守杜穆、交恥太守錫光等，相率遣使貢獻，悉封為列侯。

translations.literal: Thereupon Rang with Jiangxia governor Hou Deng, Wuling governor Wang Tang, Changsha minister Han Fu, Guilin governor Zhang Long, Lingling governor Tian Xi, Cangwu governor Du Mu, Jiaozhi governor Xi Guang, and others, in succession sent envoys with tribute; all were enfeoffed as full marquises.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Rang joined Hou Deng, Wang Tang, Han Fu, Zhang Long of Guiyang commandery, Tian Xi, Du Mu, Xi Guang of Jiaozhi, and others in sending tribute; each received a full marquisate.

sentences.zh: 或遣子將兵助彭征伐。

translations.literal: Some sent sons leading troops to aid Peng in campaigning.
translations.idiomatic: Some even sent sons with troops to fight under Cen Peng.

sentences.zh: 於是江南之珍始流通焉。

translations.literal: Thereupon the treasures of south of the Yangzi began to circulate.
translations.idiomatic: For the first time the wealth of the south flowed north to the court.

sentences.zh: 六年冬，征彭詣京師，數召宴見，厚加賞賜。

translations.literal: In the winter of the sixth year he summoned Peng to the capital; several times he called him to feasts and audiences and richly bestowed rewards.
translations.idiomatic: In the sixth winter he recalled Cen Peng to Luoyang, feasted him repeatedly, and heaped gifts on him.

sentences.zh: 復南還津鄉，有詔過家上冢，大長稱以朔望問太夫人起居。

translations.literal: He returned south to Jinxiang; there was an edict to pass his home and tend the ancestral tombs; the grand prolonger of autumn was to inquire on new and full moons after the great lady's daily peace.
translations.idiomatic: Ordered south again to Jinxiang, he was allowed to visit his home and tombs while the da chang qiu sent greetings to his mother on every new and full moon.

sentences.zh: 八年，彭引兵從車駕破天水，與吳漢圍隗囂於西域。

translations.literal: In the eighth year Peng led troops following the imperial carriage in breaking Tianshui and with Wu Han besieged Wei Ao at Xicheng.
translations.idiomatic: In year 8 he followed the emperor to reduce Tianshui and joined Wu Han in besieging Wei Ao at Xicheng.

sentences.zh: 時，公孫述將李育將兵救囂，守上邽，帝留蓋延、聯弇圍之，而車駕東歸。

translations.literal: At that time Gongsun Shu's general Li Yu led troops to rescue Ao, guarded Shanggui; the emperor left Gai Yan and Geng Yan to besiege him, while the imperial carriage returned east.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Shu's Li Yu relieved Wei Ao and held Shanggui; the emperor left Gai Yan and Geng Yan to invest the town while he rode east.

sentences.zh: 敕彭書曰：『兩城若下，便可將兵南擊蜀虜。

translations.literal: He instructed Peng in a letter, saying: 'If the two cities are taken, you may then lead troops south to strike the Shu bandits.
translations.idiomatic: He wrote, 'When those two towns fall, take your army straight at Shu.'

sentences.zh: 人若不知足，既平隴，復望蜀。

translations.literal: Men if they do not know contentment, having pacified Long, again look toward Shu.
translations.idiomatic: Men are never content: Long subdued, they already eye Shu.'

sentences.zh: 每一發兵，頭須為白。

translations.literal: Each time troops are sent, the hair on the head must turn white.
translations.idiomatic: Every campaign I launch turns another shock of my hair white.'

sentences.zh: 』彭遂壅谷水灌西城，城未沒丈餘，囂將行巡、周宗將蜀救兵到，囂得出還冀。

translations.literal: ' Peng thereupon dammed the valley waters to flood Xicheng; the city was not yet submerged more than a span when Ao's generals Xing Xun and Zhou Zong led Shu relief troops to arrive; Ao obtained exit and returned to Ji.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Peng dammed the headwaters and drowned Xicheng's walls, but before the water rose a yard Xing Xun and Zhou Zong arrived with Shu reinforcements and snatched Wei Ao back to Ji.

sentences.zh: 漢軍食盡，燒輜重，引兵下隴，延、弇亦相隨而退。

translations.literal: The Han army's food was exhausted; they burned the baggage train and led troops down Long; Yan and Yan also followed each other in retreat.
translations.idiomatic: Han supplies failed; they burned the wagons and withdrew down the Long plateau, Gai Yan and Geng Yan following suit.

sentences.zh: 囂出兵尾擊諸營，彭殿為後拒，故諸將能全師東歸。

translations.literal: Ao sent troops to tail-strike the various camps; Peng covered the rear as rear guard, therefore the various generals were able to preserve their hosts whole and return east.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Ao harried the retreat, but Cen Peng held the rear so every column returned intact.

sentences.zh: 彭還津鄉。

translations.literal: Peng returned to Jinxiang.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Peng went back to Jinxiang.

sentences.zh: 九年，公孫述遣其將任滿、田戎、程汎，將數萬人乘枋箄下江關，擊破馮駿及田鴻、李玄等。

translations.literal: In the ninth year Gongsun Shu sent his generals Ren Man, Tian Rong, and Cheng Fan, leading several tens of thousands of men, riding rafts down from Jiang Pass, struck and broke Feng Jun and Tian Hong, Li Xuan, and others.
translations.idiomatic: In year 9 Gongsun Shu dispatched Ren Man, Tian Rong, and Cheng Fan with tens of thousands down the Yangzi on rafts from Jiang Pass, defeating Feng Jun, Tian Hong, and Li Xuan.

sentences.zh: 遂拔夷道、夷陵，據荊門、虎牙。

translations.literal: They thereupon took Yidao and Yiling and held Jingmen and Huya.
translations.idiomatic: They seized Yidao and Yiling and blocked Jingmen and Huya.

sentences.zh: 橫江水起浮橋、鬥樓，立欑柱絕水道，結營山上，以拒漢兵。

translations.literal: They stretched ropes across the river, raised floating bridges and fighting towers, set pile posts to cut the waterway, and bound camps on the hills to resist Han troops.
translations.idiomatic: They spanned the river with cables, floating bridges, and watchtowers, sank piles to choke the channel, and camped on the heights against the Han fleet.

sentences.zh: 彭數攻之，不利，於是裝直進樓船、冒突露橈數千艘。

translations.literal: Peng several times attacked them without success; thereupon he fitted straight-ahead tower ships and dash-and-expose swift-oar boats to the number of several thousand vessels.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Peng assaulted them repeatedly without success, then rigged thousands of tower ships and fast assault craft.

sentences.zh: 十一年春，彭與吳漢及誅虜將軍劉隆、輔威將軍臧宮、驍騎將軍劉歆，發南陽、武陵、南郡兵，又發桂陽、零陵、長沙委輸棹卒，凡六萬餘人，騎五千匹，皆會荊門。

translations.literal: In the spring of the eleventh year Peng with Wu Han and general who slays the barbarians Liu Long, general who aids might Zang Gong, general of swift cavalry Liu Xin, raised troops of Nanyang, Wuling, and Nan commandery, and again raised from Guilin, Lingling, and Changsha transport oarsmen, in all more than sixty thousand men, five thousand horses, all assembling at Jingmen.
translations.idiomatic: In spring of year 11 he and Wu Han, Liu Long, Zang Gong, Liu Xin, and others mustered sixty thousand foot from Nanyang, Wuling, and Nan commandery, five thousand horse, and oarsmen from Guiyang, Lingling, and Changsha at Jingmen.

sentences.zh: 吳漢以三郡棹卒多費糧谷，欲罷之。

translations.literal: Wu Han, because the oarsmen of the three commanderies consumed much grain, wished to dismiss them.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Han wanted to send the three commanderies' boatmen home to save grain.

sentences.zh: 彭以蜀兵盛，不可遣，上書言狀。

translations.literal: Peng, because Shu troops were strong, said they could not be sent away; he submitted a memorial stating the situation.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Peng insisted the Shu host was too strong to cut forces and memorialized the facts.

sentences.zh: 帝報彭曰：『大司馬習用步騎，不曉水戰，荊門之事，一由征南公為重而已。

translations.literal: The emperor replied to Peng, saying: 'The grand marshal is practiced in using infantry and cavalry and does not understand water warfare; as for the matter of Jingmen, it is entirely for the grand general on the south to be responsible—that is all.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor answered, 'The grand marshal knows infantry and cavalry, not river war—everything at Jingmen rests with you, general of the south.'

sentences.zh: 』彭乃令軍中募攻浮橋，先登者上賞。

translations.literal: ' Peng thereupon ordered in the army to recruit men to attack the floating bridge; those who first mounted received upper reward.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Peng offered top bounty to whoever first stormed the floating bridge.'

sentences.zh: 於是偏將軍魯奇應募而前。

translations.literal: Thereupon lieutenant general Lu Qi answered the recruitment and went forward.
translations.idiomatic: Lieutenant General Lu Qi volunteered and led the way.

sentences.zh: 時天風狂急，奇船逆流而上，直沖浮橋，而欑柱鉤不得去，奇等乘勢殊死戰，因飛炬焚之，風怒火盛，橋樓崩燒。

translations.literal: At the time heaven's wind was violent and urgent; Qi's boats went upstream against the current, went straight to ram the floating bridge, but the pile posts hooked and could not get free; Qi and the others by the momentum fought a desperate battle, thereupon flew torches to burn them; wind was keen and fire flourished; the bridge towers collapsed and burned.
translations.idiomatic: A fierce wind blew; Lu Qi drove upstream onto the bridge until his hull snagged on the piles; his men fought to the death, hurled fire brands, and wind-whipped flames brought the towers crashing down in flame.

sentences.zh: 彭復悉軍順風並進，所向無前。

translations.literal: Peng again with the whole army followed the wind and advanced together; wherever they turned there was none ahead.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Peng then sent the whole fleet downwind and swept all before him.

sentences.zh: 蜀兵大亂，溺死者數千人。

translations.literal: Shu troops were in great disorder; those who drowned numbered several thousand.
translations.idiomatic: The Shu lines collapsed and several thousand men drowned.

sentences.zh: 斬任滿，生獲程汎，而田戎亡保江州。

translations.literal: They beheaded Ren Man, captured Cheng Fan alive, while Tian Rong fled to hold Jiangzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Ren Man died on the field, Cheng Fan was taken alive, and Tian Rong fled to fortify Jiangzhou.

sentences.zh: 彭上劉隆為南郡太守，自率臧宮、劉歆長驅入江關，令軍中無得虜掠。

translations.literal: Peng memorialized Liu Long as governor of Nan commandery and himself led Zang Gong and Liu Xin in a long drive into Jiang Pass, ordering in the army that there must be no plundering.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Peng recommended Liu Long for governor of Nan commandery, then drove deep into Jiang Pass with Zang Gong and Liu Xin under strict orders against looting.

sentences.zh: 所以，百姓皆奉牛、酒迎勞。

translations.literal: For this reason the hundred surnames all presented oxen and wine in welcome and labor.
translations.idiomatic: For that the villagers pressed cattle and wine on him in welcome.

sentences.zh: 彭見諸耆老，為言大漢哀湣巴蜀久見虜役，故興師遠伐，以討有罪，為人除害。

translations.literal: Peng saw the various elders and for them spoke of great Han's pity and compassion that Ba and Shu long had been enslaved and toiled; therefore he raised the army on a distant expedition to punish the guilty and remove harm for men.
translations.idiomatic: To the elders Cen Peng said the Han court pitied Ba and Shu, long ground down by oppressors, and had marched far to punish the guilty and lift the burden from the people.

sentences.zh: 讓不受其牛、酒。

translations.literal: He modestly declined and did not accept their oxen and wine.
translations.idiomatic: He refused their oxen and wine with courtesy.

sentences.zh: 百姓皆大喜悅，爭開門降。

translations.literal: The hundred surnames were all greatly pleased and contended to open gates and surrender.
translations.idiomatic: The common folk rejoiced and vied to open their gates in submission.

sentences.zh: 詔彭守益州牧，所下郡，輒行太守事。

translations.literal: An edict ordered Peng to act as shepherd of Yi province; in whatever commandery he descended, he at once exercised the shepherd's affairs.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor named Cen Peng acting governor of Yi: in every county he took he exercised a governor's authority.

sentences.zh: 彭到江州，以田戎食多，難卒拔，留馮駿守之，自引兵乘利直指墊江，攻破平曲，收其米數十萬石。

translations.literal: When Peng reached Jiangzhou, because Tian Rong had much food and was hard to take quickly, he left Feng Jun to guard him and himself led troops riding advantage straight to Dianjiang, broke Pingqu, and collected their grain to the number of several hundred thousand shi.
translations.idiomatic: At Jiangzhou Cen Peng saw Tian Rong too well supplied for a quick siege; he left Feng Jun to pin him while he struck straight for Dianjiang, stormed Pingqu, and seized hundreds of thousands of bushels of grain.

sentences.zh: 公孫述使其將延岑、呂鮪、王元及其弟恢悉兵拒廣漢及資中，又遣將侯丹率二萬餘人拒黃石。

translations.literal: Gongsun Shu sent his generals Yan Cen, Lü Wei, Wang Yuan, and his younger brother Hui with their whole force to resist Guanghan and Zizhong, and again sent general Hou Dan with more than twenty thousand men to hold Huangshi.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Shu threw Yan Cen, Lü Wei, Wang Yuan, and Wang Hui against Guanghan and Zizhong while Hou Dan blocked Huangshi with twenty thousand men.

sentences.zh: 彭乃多張疑兵，使護軍楊翕與臧宮拒延岑等，自分兵浮江下還江州，溯都江而上，襲擊侯丹，大破之。

translations.literal: Peng thereupon greatly spread doubtful troops, had protector of the army Yang Xi with Zang Gong resist Yan Cen and the others, himself divided troops to float down the river back to Jiangzhou, went up the Dujiang current, raided and struck Hou Dan, and greatly defeated him.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Peng feigned deployments, left Yang Xi and Zang Gong to tie down Yan Cen, doubled back downriver to Jiangzhou, then swept up the Min to ambush Hou Dan and crush him.

sentences.zh: 因晨夜倍道兼行二千餘里，徑拔武陽。

translations.literal: Therefore morning and night he doubled the road and marched more than two thousand li in all, went straight and took Wuyang.
translations.idiomatic: He then drove night and day over two thousand li and seized Wuyang in a single rush.

sentences.zh: 使精騎馳廣都，去成都數十里，勢若風雨，所至皆奔散。

translations.literal: He sent crack cavalry to gallop to Guangdu, several tens of li from Chengdu; the momentum was like wind and rain; wherever they arrived all fled and scattered.
translations.idiomatic: He sent horsemen racing to Guangdu, within a few dozen li of Chengdu; they swept like a storm and every garrison broke before them.

sentences.zh: 初，述聞漢兵在平曲，故遣大兵逆之。

translations.literal: At first, when Shu heard that Han troops were at Pingqu, for that reason he sent a great army to meet them.
translations.idiomatic: Shu had massed troops to meet the Han at Pingqu.

sentences.zh: 及彭至武陽，繞出延岑軍後，蜀地震駭。

translations.literal: When Peng reached Wuyang, he went around behind Yan Cen's army; Shu was shaken in terror.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Peng appeared at Wuyang behind Yan Cen's line, and all Shu trembled.

sentences.zh: 述大驚，以杖擊地曰：『是何神也！』

translations.literal: Shu was greatly alarmed, struck the ground with his staff, and said: 'What spirit is this!'
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Shu beat his staff on the earth and cried, 'What kind of spirit is this man!'

sentences.zh: 彭所營地名彭亡，聞而惡之，欲徙，會日暮，蜀刺客詐為亡奴降，夜刺殺彭。

translations.literal: The place where Peng encamped was named Peng's Death; hearing it he hated it and wished to move, but it happened to be dusk; a Shu assassin pretended to be a fugitive slave surrendering and by night stabbed Peng to death.
translations.idiomatic: The camp lay at a place called Pengwang—'Peng's doom'—which unnerved him; at dusk a Shu agent feigned desertion, slipped in by night, and assassinated him.

sentences.zh: 子遵嗣，徒封細陽侯。

translations.literal: The son Zun inherited; his fief was moved to marquis of Xiyang.
translations.idiomatic: His son Cen Zun succeeded, with the title shifted to Xiyang.

sentences.zh: 十三年，帝思彭功，復封遵弟淮為穀陽侯。

translations.literal: In the thirteenth year the emperor thought of Peng's merit and again enfeoffed Zun's younger brother Huai as marquis of Guyang.
translations.idiomatic: In the thirteenth year the court remembered Cen Peng and also ennobled Zun's brother Huai as marquis of Guyang.

sentences.zh: 遵永平中為屯騎校尉。

translations.literal: Zun in the Yongping period was colonel of the garrison cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: Under Yongping Cen Zun served as colonel of the garrison cavalry.

sentences.zh: 遵卒，子伉嗣。

translations.literal: Zun died; his son Kang inherited.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Zun died and his son Cen Kang inherited.

sentences.zh: 伉卒，子杞嗣，元初三年，坐事失國。

translations.literal: Kang died; his son Qi inherited; in the third year of Yuanchu, because of an affair he lost the state.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Kang was followed by Cen Qi, who lost the fief in Yuanchu 3 for a crime.

sentences.zh: 建光元年，安帝復封杞細陽侯，順帝時為光祿勛。

translations.literal: In the first year of Jianguang the Emperor An again enfeoffed Qi as marquis of Xiyang; in the time of Shun Di he was minister of the imperial household.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor An restored Cen Qi to Xiyang in Jianguang 1; under Shun he rose to minister of the imperial household.

sentences.zh: 杞卒，子熙嗣，尚安帝妹涅陽長公主。

translations.literal: Qi died; his son Xi inherited; he married the Princess of Great Length of Nieyang, younger sister of Emperor An.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Qi was succeeded by Cen Xi, who married Emperor An's sister, the senior princess of Nieyang.

sentences.zh: 少為侍中、虎賁中郎將，朝廷多稱其能。

translations.literal: In youth he was attendant at the palace and colonel of the tiger brave guard; the court often praised his ability.
translations.idiomatic: Young, he served as palace attendant and colonel of the tiger brave guard, and the court praised his talent.

sentences.zh: 遷魏郡太守，招聘隱逸，與參政事，無為而化。

translations.literal: He was promoted governor of Wei commandery, recruited recluses, and had them join in governing affairs; by non-action there was transformation.
translations.idiomatic: As governor of Wei he called in hidden scholars to share administration and ruled so lightly that order came of itself.

sentences.zh: 視事二年，輿人歌之曰：『我有枳棘，岑君伐之。

translations.literal: In the second year of his holding office the carriage men sang of him, saying: 'We had bramble and thorn; Lord Cen cut them down.
translations.idiomatic: After two years the people sang: 'Thorns choked our fields till Lord Cen cleared them away.'

sentences.zh: 我有蟊賊，岑君遏之。

translations.literal: We had grain-eating pests; Lord Cen checked them.
translations.idiomatic: Locusts devoured our grain till Lord Cen drove them off.'

sentences.zh: 狗吠不驚，足下生氂。

translations.literal: Dogs barked without alarm; fluff grew under the feet.
translations.idiomatic: Dogs barked at ease and soft grass grew underfoot.'

sentences.zh: 含哺鼓腹，焉知凶災？

translations.literal: With food in the mouth they patted their bellies—how should they know misfortune and disaster?
translations.idiomatic: Men fed at leisure, patting full bellies—who thought of sorrow?'

sentences.zh: 我喜我生，獨丁斯時。

translations.literal: I rejoice in my life, alone encountering this time.
translations.idiomatic: We bless our lives to have lived in such an age.'

sentences.zh: 美矣岑君，於戲休茲！』

translations.literal: How beautiful Lord Cen—ah, rest in this!'
translations.idiomatic: How fine is Lord Cen—may his grace never end!'

sentences.zh: 熙卒，子福嗣，為黃門侍郎。

translations.literal: Xi died; his son Fu inherited; he was gentleman at the yellow gate.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Xi was succeeded by Cen Fu, who served as gentleman at the yellow gate.

sentences.zh: 賈復字君文，南陽冠軍人也。

translations.literal: Jia Fu, courtesy name Junwen, was a man of Guanjun in Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Fu, styled Junwen, came from Guanjun in Nanyang.

sentences.zh: 少好學，習《尚書》。

translations.literal: In youth he loved learning and studied the Book of Documents.
translations.idiomatic: As a boy he loved books and mastered the Book of Documents.

sentences.zh: 事舞陰李生，李生奇之，謂門人曰：『賈君之容貌志氣如此，而勤於學，將相之器也。

translations.literal: He studied under Mr Li of Wuyin; Li marveled at him and said to his disciples: 'Master Jia's bearing and resolve are thus, and he is diligent in learning—he has the capacity of a general or minister.
translations.idiomatic: He studied under a teacher Li at Wuyin, who told his pupils, 'Jia Fu has the look and drive of a commander yet keeps at his books—he will be general or minister material.'

sentences.zh: 』王莽末，為縣掾，迎鹽河東，會遇盜賊，等比十餘人皆放散其鹽，復獨完以還縣，縣中稱其信。

translations.literal: ' At the end of Wang Mang he was a clerk of the county, escorting salt east of the river; it happened he met bandits; more than ten companions in the party all scattered their salt; Fu alone brought it whole back to the county; the county praised his trustworthiness.
translations.idiomatic: Near Wang Mang's fall he was a county clerk fetching salt east of the river when bandits struck; a dozen men dumped their loads, but Jia Fu brought every sack home intact and won the county's praise for honesty.

sentences.zh: 時，下江、新市兵起，復亦聚眾數百人於羽山，自號將軍。

translations.literal: At that time the Lower Yangzi and Xinshi forces rose; Fu also gathered a host of several hundred men at Mount Yu and styled himself general.
translations.idiomatic: When the Xinshi and Lower Yangzi rebels rose, Jia Fu rallied several hundred men on Mount Yu and called himself a general.

sentences.zh: 更始立，乃將其眾歸漢中王劉嘉，以為校尉。

translations.literal: When Gengshi was established, he then led his host to submit to Prince of Hanzhong Liu Jia and was made colonel.
translations.idiomatic: After Gengshi took the throne he brought his band to Liu Jia, prince of Hanzhong, who named him colonel.

sentences.zh: 復見更始政亂，諸將放縱，乃說嘉曰：『臣聞圖堯、舜之事而不能至者，湯、武是也；

translations.literal: Fu saw that Gengshi's government was in disorder and the generals gave free rein; thereupon he persuaded Jia, saying: 'Your servant has heard that those who plan the deeds of Yao and Shun yet cannot reach them are Tang and Wu;
translations.idiomatic: Seeing Gengshi's court rot and his generals run wild, Jia Fu urged Liu Jia: 'Those who aim at Yao and Shun yet fall short become founders like Tang and Wu;'

sentences.zh: 圖湯、武之事而不能至者，桓、文是也；

translations.literal: those who plan the deeds of Tang and Wu yet cannot reach them are Huan and Wen;
translations.idiomatic: those who aim at Tang and Wu yet fall short become hegemons like Huan and Wen;'

sentences.zh: 圖桓、文之事而不能至者，六國是也；

translations.literal: those who plan the deeds of Huan and Wen yet cannot reach them are the six states;
translations.idiomatic: aim at hegemony like Duke Huan or Duke Wen yet fall short, and you end among the doomed alliances of the east;'

sentences.zh: 定六國之規，欲安守之而不能至者，亡六國是也。

translations.literal: those who fixed the regulations of the six states, wishing peacefully to guard them yet could not reach it, are they who destroyed the six states.
translations.idiomatic: try only to preserve a league of states in peace—and fail—and you share the fate of the realms Qin extinguished.'

sentences.zh: 今漢室中興，大王以親戚為藩輔，天下未定而安守所保，所保得無不可保乎？

translations.literal: Now the house of Han is restored at its center; the great king, as kin, is a screen and helper; under heaven is not yet settled yet you peacefully guard what you protect—can what you protect fail to be what cannot be protected?
translations.idiomatic: The Han revives at its heart while you, royal kin, lean on a rich domain; the empire is still unsettled—can any fence you think safe stay standing forever?'

sentences.zh: 』嘉曰：『卿言大，非吾任也。

translations.literal: ' Jia said: 'Your words are great; they are not what I can undertake.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Jia answered, 'That is too large a burden for me.'

sentences.zh: 大司馬劉公在河北，必能相施，第持我書往。

translations.literal: The grand marshal Liu is north of the Yellow River; he is sure able to employ you; only take my letter and go.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Marshal Liu is in Hebei and will know how to use you—take my letter north.'

sentences.zh: 』復遂辭嘉，受書北度河，及光武於柏人，因鄧禹得召見。

translations.literal: ' Fu thereupon took leave of Jia, received the letter, crossed north of the Yellow River, and when he reached Guangwu at Bairen, through Deng Yu obtained summons for audience.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Fu left Liu Jia, crossed the Yellow River with the letter, and met Guangwu at Bairen through Deng Yu.

sentences.zh: 光武奇之，禹亦稱有將帥節，於是署復破虜將軍督盜賊。

translations.literal: Guangwu marveled at him; Yu also praised him as having the bearing of a general or commander; thereupon he appointed Fu general who breaks the barbarians, supervisor of bandits.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu was impressed; Deng Yu praised his commander's bearing, and he was named general who breaks the barbarians and supervisor of bandits.

sentences.zh: 復馬羸，光武解左驂以賜之。

translations.literal: Fu's horses were lean; Guangwu undid the left trace-horse and bestowed it on him.
translations.idiomatic: His horses were poor, so Guangwu unhitched his own left trace-horse and gave it to him.

sentences.zh: 官屬以復後來而好陵折等輩，調補鄗尉，光武曰：『賈督有折沖千里之威，方任以職，勿得擅除。』

translations.literal: Subordinates, because Fu came later yet liked to humiliate and break equals, transferred him to be sheriff of Gao; Guangwu said: 'Supervisor Jia has the might to repel the enemy a thousand li; I am just now entrusting him with office—do not remove him on your own.'
translations.idiomatic: Staff tried to demote the latecomer who bullied his peers to sheriff of Gao; Guangwu snapped, 'Jia Fu can turn an army at a thousand li—assign him real duty and do not demote him at will.'

sentences.zh: 光武至信都，以復為偏將軍。

translations.literal: When Guangwu reached Xindu, he made Fu lieutenant general.
translations.idiomatic: At Xindu he made Jia Fu lieutenant general.

sentences.zh: 及拔邯鄲，遷都護將軍。

translations.literal: When Handan was taken, he was promoted general who protects all.
translations.idiomatic: After Handan fell he became general who protects all.

sentences.zh: 從擊青犢於射犬，大戰至日中，賊陳堅不卻。

translations.literal: He followed in striking the Green Calves at Shequan; the great battle reached midday; the bandit formation was firm and would not yield.
translations.idiomatic: He fought the Green Calves at Shequan until noon against a line that would not break.

sentences.zh: 光武傳召復曰：『吏士皆饑，可且朝飯。

translations.literal: Guangwu passed word summoning Fu, saying: 'Officers and soldiers are all hungry; you may for the moment take morning meal.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu sent word: 'The men are starving—break off for breakfast.'

sentences.zh: 』復曰：『先破之，然後食耳！

translations.literal: ' Fu said: 'First break them, then eat!
translations.idiomatic: Jia Fu shouted back, 'Break them first, then we eat!'

sentences.zh: 』於是被羽先登，所向皆靡，賊乃敗走。

translations.literal: ' Thereupon bearing plumes he mounted first; wherever he turned all fell; the bandits thereupon were defeated and fled.
translations.idiomatic: He seized his shield, led the storming party, shattered the enemy, and sent them running.

sentences.zh: 諸將咸服其勇。

translations.literal: The generals all admired his courage.
translations.idiomatic: Every general stood in awe of his valor.

sentences.zh: 又北與五校戰於真定，大破之。

translations.literal: Again north he with the Five Schools fought at Zhending and greatly defeated them.
translations.idiomatic: He next crushed the Five Schools at Zhending.

sentences.zh: 復傷創甚。

translations.literal: Fu's wound was very severe.
translations.idiomatic: He took grievous wounds.

sentences.zh: 光武大驚曰：『我所以不令賈復別將者，為其輕敵也。

translations.literal: Guangwu was greatly alarmed and said: 'The reason I did not order Jia Fu to command a separate force is that he despises the enemy.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu cried, 'I kept Jia Fu from independent command because he scorns danger too much.'

sentences.zh: 果然，失吾名將。

translations.literal: As expected, I have lost my famous general.
translations.idiomatic: And now I have lost my finest captain.'

sentences.zh: 聞其婦有孕，生女邪，我子娶之，生男邪，我女嫁之，不令其憂妻子也。

translations.literal: I hear his wife is with child—if it is a girl, my son shall marry her; if it is a boy, my daughter shall marry him—I will not let him worry about wife and children.
translations.idiomatic: If his wife bears a girl my son will wed her; if a boy my daughter will marry him—his family will never want while I live.'

sentences.zh: 』復病尋愈，追及光武於薊，相見甚歡，大饗士卒，令復居前，擊鄴賊，破之。

translations.literal: ' Fu's illness soon healed; he caught up with Guangwu at Ji; they met with great joy; he feasted the soldiers greatly, ordered Fu to go in front, struck the Ye bandits, and broke them.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Fu mended, rejoined Guangwu at Ji to great rejoicing, feasted the troops, and led the van to crush the Ye rebels.'

sentences.zh: 復從征伐，未嘗喪敗，數與諸將潰圍解急，身被十二創。

translations.literal: Fu followed in campaigns and never met defeat; several times with the various generals he broke encirclements and relieved urgency; on his body he received twelve wounds.
translations.idiomatic: He campaigned without defeat, broke sieges beside his comrades, and bore twelve wounds.

sentences.zh: 帝以復敢深入，希令遠征，而壯其勇節，常自從之，故復少方面之勛。

translations.literal: The emperor, because Fu dared to go deep, rarely ordered distant expeditions, yet admired his courage and integrity and often had him follow himself; therefore Fu had few achievements in holding a quarter.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor, knowing his reckless dash, seldom sent him far afield yet kept him near out of respect—so he won fewer independent commands.

sentences.zh: 諸將每論功自伐，復未嘗有言。

translations.literal: Whenever the generals discussed merit and boasted of themselves, Fu never had words.
translations.idiomatic: When others bragged of their feats Jia Fu stayed silent.

sentences.zh: 帝輒曰：『賈君之功，我自知之。』

translations.literal: The emperor always said: 'Master Jia's merit, I myself know it.'
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu would say, 'Jia Fu's deeds—I keep the tally myself.'

sentences.zh: 十三年，定封膠東侯，食郁秩、壯武、下密、即墨、梃、觀陽，凡六縣。

translations.literal: In the thirteenth year his fief was fixed as marquis of Jiaodong, with food income from Yuzhi, Zhuangwu, Xiami, Jimo, Ting, and Guanyang, in all six counties.
translations.idiomatic: In the thirteenth year he received the Jiaodong marquisate with six counties' revenue.

sentences.zh: 復知帝欲偃干戈，修文德，不欲功臣擁眾京師，乃與高密侯鄧禹並剽甲兵，敦儒學。

translations.literal: Fu knew the emperor wished to lay down weapons of war and cultivate civil virtue and did not wish meritorious ministers to hold masses of troops at the capital; thereupon together with Marquis of Gaomi Deng Yu he both stripped armor and weapons and urged Confucian learning.
translations.idiomatic: Seeing the emperor meant to sheathe the sword and rule by culture, not by captains camped at Luoyang, he and Deng Yu of Gaomi stacked their arms and took up the classics.

sentences.zh: 帝深然之，遂罷左右將軍。

translations.literal: The emperor deeply approved this; thereupon he dismissed the left and right generals.
translations.idiomatic: The throne approved and abolished the left and right generals' posts.

sentences.zh: 復以列侯就第，加位特進。

translations.literal: Fu as full marquis went to his mansion and was given supernumerary rank of special advancement.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Fu retired to his estate as a full marquis with the added rank of special advancement.

sentences.zh: 復為人剛毅方直，多大節。

translations.literal: Fu was by nature firm, resolute, square, and straight, with much of great principle.
translations.idiomatic: He was stern, blunt, and built for great occasions.

sentences.zh: 既還私第，闔門養威重。

translations.literal: When he had returned to his private mansion, he closed his gates and nurtured awe-inspiring weight.
translations.idiomatic: At home he shut his gates and cultivated a quiet dignity.

sentences.zh: 朱祐等薦復宜為宰相，帝方以吏事責三公，故功臣並不用。

translations.literal: Zhu You and others recommended Fu as fitting to be chancellor; the emperor was at that time charging the three dukes with administrative affairs, therefore meritorious ministers were all not employed.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu You urged making him chancellor, but the emperor was loading the three excellencies with routine business and would not give such posts to old warriors.

sentences.zh: 是時，列侯惟高密、固始、膠東三侯與公卿參議國家大事，恩遇甚厚。

translations.literal: At that time among full marquises only the three marquises of Gaomi, Gushi, and Jiaodong joined the dukes and ministers in deliberating great affairs of state; favor and treatment were very thick.
translations.idiomatic: Only Deng Yu of Gaomi, Fu of Gushi, and Jia Fu of Jiaodong sat with the ministers on state policy—such was their favor.

sentences.zh: 三十一年卒，謚曰剛侯。

translations.literal: In the thirty-first year he died; posthumous title Marquis Gang.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the thirty-first year of the reign, posthumously Marquis Gang.

sentences.zh: 子忠嗣。

translations.literal: The son Zhong inherited.
translations.idiomatic: His son Jia Zhong succeeded.

sentences.zh: 忠卒，子敏嗣。

translations.literal: Zhong died; his son Min inherited.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Zhong was followed by Jia Min.

sentences.zh: 建初元年，坐誣告母殺人。

translations.literal: In the first year of Jianchu, because he falsely accused his mother of killing a man.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianchu 1 Jia Min was convicted of slandering his mother for murder.

sentences.zh: 國除。

translations.literal: The state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: The fief was stripped.

sentences.zh: 肅宗更封復小子邯為膠東侯，邯弟宗為即墨侯，各一縣。

translations.literal: Suzong again enfeoffed Fu's youngest son Han as marquis of Jiaodong, Han's younger brother Zong as marquis of Jimo, each one county.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Zhang re-enfeoffed Jia Fu's younger son Han at Jiaodong and Han's brother Zong at Jimo, each with one county.

sentences.zh: 邯卒，子育嗣。

translations.literal: Han died; his son Yu inherited.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Han died; Jia Yu inherited.

sentences.zh: 育卒，子長嗣。

translations.literal: Yu died; his son Zhang inherited.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Yu was followed by Jia Zhang.

sentences.zh: 宗字武孺，少有操行，多智略。

translations.literal: Zong, courtesy name Wuru, in youth had conduct and integrity and much clever strategy.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Zong, styled Wuru, was principled and resourceful from boyhood.

sentences.zh: 初拜郎中，稍遷，建初中為朔方太守。

translations.literal: At first he was appointed gentleman of the palace; gradually he was promoted; in the middle of Jianchu he was governor of Shuofang.
translations.idiomatic: He rose from gentleman of the palace to governor of Shuofang in mid Jianchu.

sentences.zh: 舊內郡徙人在邊者，率多貧弱，為居人所仆役，不得為吏。

translations.literal: Formerly migrants from inner commanderies who were moved to the border were mostly poor and weak and were enslaved by the inhabitants, unable to become clerks.
translations.idiomatic: Colonists sent from the interior were usually poor, bullied by locals, and barred from office.

sentences.zh: 宗擢用其任職者，與邊吏參選，轉相監司，以擿發其奸，或以功次補長吏，故各願盡死。

translations.literal: Zong promoted and employed those among them fit for office, joined them in selection with border clerks, turned and supervised one another to expose their wickedness, or by merit order supplemented senior clerks, therefore each was willing to exhaust death.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Zong promoted able colonists, rotated them with native officials in mutual oversight, exposed corruption, and advanced men by merit so that all fought to the death for him.

sentences.zh: 匈奴畏之，不敢入塞。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu feared him and did not dare enter the passes.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu feared him and dared not raid the frontier.

sentences.zh: 徵為長水校尉。

translations.literal: He was summoned to be colonel of the long waters.
translations.idiomatic: He was recalled as colonel of the long waters.

sentences.zh: 宗兼通儒術，每宴見，常使與少府丁鴻等論議於前。

translations.literal: Zong was also versed in Confucian learning; whenever there were feasts and audiences he always had him with junior minister Ding Hong and others debate before him.
translations.idiomatic: Learned in the classics, he was often called with Ding Hong of the privy treasury to debate policy at court feasts.

sentences.zh: 章和二年卒，朝廷湣惜焉。

translations.literal: In the second year of Zhanghe he died; the court pitied and regretted him.
translations.idiomatic: He died in Zhanghe 2 to the court's deep regret.

sentences.zh: 子參嗣。

translations.literal: The son Can inherited.
translations.idiomatic: His son Jia Can succeeded.

sentences.zh: 參卒，子建嗣。

translations.literal: Can died; his son Jian inherited.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Can was followed by Jia Jian.

sentences.zh: 元初元年，尚和帝女臨潁長公主。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yuanchu he married the Princess of Great Length of Linying, daughter of Emperor He.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanchu 1 he married Emperor He's daughter, the senior princess of Linying.

sentences.zh: 主兼食潁陰、許，合三縣，數萬戶。

translations.literal: The princess concurrently had food income from Yinyin and Xu, three counties in all, several tens of thousands of households.
translations.idiomatic: Her appanage added Yinyin and Xu—three counties and tens of thousands of households.

sentences.zh: 時鄧太后臨朝，光寵最盛，以建為侍中，順帝時為光祿勛。

translations.literal: At that time Empress Dowager Deng held court; glory and favor were at their height; she made Jian attendant at the palace; in the time of Shun Di he was minister of the imperial household.
translations.idiomatic: Under Empress Dowager Deng's regency the house blazed with favor; Jia Jian became palace attendant and later minister of the household under Shun.

sentences.zh: 論曰：中興將帥立功名者眾矣，惟岑彭、馮異建方面之號，自函谷以西，方城以南，兩將之功，實為大焉。

translations.literal: The appraisal says: Among the generals and commanders of the restoration who established merit and fame there are many; only Cen Peng and Feng Yi received titles as holders of a quarter; from west of Hangu Pass to south of Fangcheng, the merit of the two generals was truly great.
translations.idiomatic: The historian remarks: many captains won glory in the restoration, yet only Cen Peng and Feng Yi held full regional commands; west of Hangu and south of Fangcheng their achievement towered above the rest.

sentences.zh: 若馮、賈之不伐，岑公之義信，乃足以感三軍而懷敵人，故能克成遠業，終全其慶也。

translations.literal: As for Feng and Jia's not boasting and Lord Cen's righteousness and trustworthiness—they were enough to move the three armies and cherish the enemy; therefore they could complete distant enterprise and in the end preserve their good fortune.
translations.idiomatic: Feng Yi and Jia Fu's modesty and Cen Peng's good faith could move their own armies and disarm foes—hence they finished great campaigns and kept their blessings whole.

sentences.zh: 昔高祖忌柏人之名，違之以全福；

translations.literal: In antiquity Gaozu grudged the name Bairen and avoided it to preserve good fortune;
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu once shunned an ill-omened place name and so escaped harm;'

sentences.zh: 征南惡彭亡之地，留之以生災。

translations.literal: the southern expedition hated the place Peng's Death and stayed there to breed disaster.
translations.idiomatic: the southern commander lingered at Pengwang—'Peng's doom'—and met disaster there.'

sentences.zh: 豈幾慮自有明惑，將期數使之然乎？

translations.literal: Is it that forethought has its own clarity and confusion, or will fate and number make it so?
translations.idiomatic: Was that wisdom or folly—or simply fate?'

sentences.zh: 贊曰：陽夏師克，實在和德。

translations.literal: The encomium says: At Yangxia the army conquered—truly through harmony and virtue.
translations.idiomatic: The verse runs: 'Yangxia fell to harmony and virtue.'

sentences.zh: 膠東鹽吏，征南宛賊。

translations.literal: The salt clerk of Jiaodong, the southern expedition's bandit of Wan.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Fu the salt clerk, Cen Peng the scourge of Wan.'

sentences.zh: 奇鋒震敵，遠圖謀國。

translations.literal: Strange edge shook the foe; far plans mapped the state.
translations.idiomatic: Keen blades broke the enemy; long strategy built the realm.'
